sentences.text: Yeah.
Yes.
This is Wednesday March 6, 1996.
I'm Michael Kuelker, interviewing Renate Vambery in St.
Louis, Missouri.
OK.

I studied occupational therapy on a full scholarship at Washington University from beginning of '38-- they gave me some credit for my schooling in-- in Switzerland-- psychology and education and things like that.
And graduated in 1940.
That is, I still had to do what we called clinical field work or an internship.
And was not-- I couldn't become a registered occupational therapist until 1941, but I was, for all practical purposes, had my degree from Wash U by that time-- from Washington University.
And my first job, first I was then as a student to a state hospital in Nebraska and they then asked me to stay on a job there to direct the men's workshop, which I did.
And then I-- I didn't want to stay in Nebraska.
I thought it was the end of the road, practically.
It was-- was pretty desolate.
And as a matter of fact, I-- we used to take the patients outside and we did recreational therapy.
And I have a little vignette to tell about that when one of the patients, great big-- most them were farmers.
He was a great big man named Ashley-- he was very nice looking, I remember.
And he started to run away.
And we were told if you lose a patient you lose your job.
So I ran after him through the cornfield, and when I finally caught up-- it was a very hot summer day-- he said to me he would come back if I gave him a kiss or allowed him to kiss me.
And I said-- I was a student, and I said, OK, because I wanted to get him back.
I didn't want to lose my job.
So that's how I got him back and to come to join the group again.
And then I went to Chicago.
I had an offer at Michael Reese Hospital, a psychiatric unit in Chicago, and took a job there.
And I wanted to-- the war broke out as you know.
I was still in Nebraska when the war-- in '41, when we declared war.
And I remember that we-- I had read or was-- or was announced on the radio that people who were not citizens, who came from Germany were enemy aliens.
I was an enemy alien all of a sudden, and I had to turn in my camera, and my parents had to turn in their shortwave radio and their camera.
And so I went to the sheriff's office and forget-- this is out in the country in Nebraska, and he hadn't heard about it.
And he said, why do you want to give me your camera?
And I said, because I'm supposed to.
I'm an enemy alien.
And so I did and he was very nice, sent it back to me when I got my citizenship papers.
And so I wanted to be in the army, but I couldn't because I wasn't a citizen.
The boys could join and become citizens immediately, almost immediately.
They could join the army, volunteer.
Many of the young men I knew did that who had come from Austria or Germany.
And so but women couldn't do that at the time.
So in '43, I finally got my citizenship papers, and I immediately signed up.
And we were assigned-- we were had a special status-- Women's Medical Specialists Corps.
We were signed by the Surgeon General's office directly to various places.
And so I got my first assignment to Schick General Hospital in Iowa-- Clinton, Iowa.
And I-- I was sent there, I think it was-- I got my papers in September, I think, '43.
And it took me longer than it should have because I had moved around and I had to have letters of reference from everywhere I had been.
And I signed up and it was, I think, January that I was sent to Clinton, Iowa.
And after I was there for about a year.
I established an occupational therapy department.
A lot of my friends had been sent to eastern hospitals, eastern army hospitals, and so I asked the Surgeon General's office if I could be sent to one of the eastern hospitals.
And I got my orders, 16-- I don't know how you-- you know how to say in triplicate, but I don't know-- it was 16 copies-- that I was sent to Walla Walla, Washington.
And that was east of the Pacific Ocean.
That's why-- that's one of the reasons.
[LAUGHS] And I had no idea where it was.
I had to find out and so on, but that's where I was assigned so I went.
And then when that closed, at the end of the war I was sent to Fort Lewis, Washington.
And I had opened a department at Walla Walla, McCaw General Hospital.
And Fort Lewis there were already were one or two departments.
They had many.
They had a convalescence handle, they had a general hospital.
It was a big place.
And I was there, stayed there until 1949 as an occupational therapist.
And then I wanted to come back to the Midwest because my mother was not well and I felt I wanted to be back in the area at least.
I didn't particularly want to come to St.
Louis, but I applied to Elgin State Hospital.
I did like psychiatric occupational therapy better than the physical part.
And took the job as chief occupational therapist at Elgin State Hospital.
I stayed there for five years, and then, when I was married, moved to Chicago and then to Gary, Indiana.
Lived in Gary, that's where my godson was born.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took-- well, I worked for five years-- I had sort of established myself-- I thought five years was long enough, at that time, to be in a job.
Later on I realized I-- I had a vested interest in retirement, but at that time I didn't think about that.
And so I moved, then, to Gary, Indiana and lived there for a couple of years.
And then we lived in Chicago where I was married.
And I drove every day from Chicago to Gary to my job, which was-- then heading a-- a clinic actually, for preschool handicapped children.
And since I had had training in early childhood education, I thought I would do that, combine my knowledge in child psychology and so on, and direct that clinic.
Which was a good experience.
It was in Gary, but it was a long drive from Chicago to Gary, but my husband was working in Chicago.
He was not well, already, at the time, and had a heart condition of which he then died.
And so when he died and I felt I wanted to go to graduate school-- I had been taking courses actually, evening courses, late afternoon at University of Chicago, graduate courses.
And so I decided I could do my graduate studies as well in St.
Louis, help my parents at the time, same time, since I was widowed then.
And so I moved back in 1960, and went to for two years to Washington University graduate school, got a master's in psychology.
And then we worked at Jewish Employment and Vocational Services-- it's now called Metropolitan Employment Services-- for five years as a senior counseling psychologist.
I did a lot of testing and so on.
And then that time, the Jewish employment-- Jewish agencies did not pay women as well as men, was still-- in the 60s, there's still stigma.
And so I went to Malcolm Bliss Mental Health Center Bay Hospital as a rehabilitation specialist.
And then I realized I can't leave after five years anymore.
I have to have retirement.
So I stayed 10 years there until I took early retirement because I-- my bad back.
I have an implant in my back.
And so it was a long career but I also did a lot of other things.

Since we mentioned ethical society earlier, I became very involved and created a committee of concern which concerned itself with older or sick members, people who were in need of some personal attention.
I used to visit everybody in the hospital.
We used to get groceries to people who came home from the hospitals or whatever was needed.
And I established a taxi service for people who could no longer drive or were afraid to drive.
It was also at night.

And had to justify to our lawyers that we could use our relief fund because I felt that intellectual stimulation and socialization was as important to people in old age-- I recognized that in the early '70s when I was not a senior citizen, yet-- was as important as eating or drinking or sleeping.
And so we were allowed to use the money, the interest from a fund, a relief fund, for a taxi service.
Yes.
Yeah, right.
Oh, very much so.
You know, I remember very much the D-Day and V-E Day and all those.
I was still in the service.
I was still at Fort Lewis at that time.
And I, of course, knew a lot of soldiers-- we had the soldiers who came back.
We had a lot of people coming back from the Pacific.

And as much as I have been opposed to nuclear-- to the bomb, atomic bomb and nuclear war in general nuclear, I can't forget the pictures of the soldiers that I worked with who came back paralyzed, totally-- you know, extremely bad shape some of them.
Some of them who told their fiances that they couldn't marry them and to just forget about them and things like that.
It was, I think, very, very difficult.
And then I have felt-- I used to go to our celebration-- well, it was not a real celebration-- against the nuclear bombing, against the atomic bomb.

On August 6th we would-- the Hiroshima bombing used to be celebrated here locally-- and I haven't gone anymore the last two or three years-- because I was thinking of the injured soldiers that I worked with, that I saw.
So, you know, the Japanese were not totally innocent as some would want you to believe.
You know, after all, Pearl Harbor, they did start Pearl Harbor.
So I had some really mixed feelings.
I don't believe we should be killing people any place, any time, actually.
But I think we have a-- a right to defend ourselves.
I see.
Did news reports or personal accounts in any way dealing with the Holocaust filter through to where you were during or shortly after the war?
Yes.
Well, when I found out that my aunt and uncle had been murdered, that-- we actually, we knew-- I don't think we knew, I knew or anyone else that I knew the extent of the concentration camps.
I was-- I don't think I was aware of that, to what extent and how many people had been murdered until later.
But I knew that people could no longer get out, could no longer come into this country.
And when I found out that the children's transport had been denied entrance to the United States-- that I found out early on-- I don't remember through what sources that became known.
There had been-- and the the ship, that was sent back.
I have a second cousin still living in Chicago whose parents were on that ship and were then murdered-- had to go back.
And she had scrubbed floors in New York to earn enough money to get them, for the fare.
They were supposed to go to Cuba, you know, the ship, and Cuba didn't want them.
It was all a-- a scam, you know, that they paid and this guy didn't have any authority or whatever to let them into Cuba.
So that I knew, I guess-- well, that was in '39, I believe.
And when I was still living in Chicago, working in Chicago before I joined the service, I was going around collecting for a bomber from a German-Jewish refugees.
Got enough money together to buy a bomber to bomb Germany.
So I had thought of myself as a pacifist.
My mother had thought of herself as a pacifist, but under the circumstances we gave up being pure pacifists because we felt that Hitler had to go and so that had to be stopped.
So I have said to a lot of people, a lot of my friends are pacifists, that I can't be that anymore, pure pacifist.
I feel we have a right to defend ourselves.
I see.
You told me an interesting narrative that I'd like you to reprise if you don't mind about a return to Stuttgart in 1967.
Yes.
Right.
My first time, after both my parents had died, and I had been divorced-- my second husband-- I went back to-- in 1967.
That was 30 years after I had-- I had left Germany.
And I said, I finally found the courage to return to my, quote, 'fatherland,' my homeland, in my old hometown, Stuttgart.
It was an attempt to go home to find myself after going through a divorce and experiencing the death of both parents within three months of each other.
And I felt the need-- I won't read everything on that.
There was one classmate I write about who I could not find her, who had sent flowers to the ship, to the boat what we left on the for me.
And I was not-- she was not a close friend, but I couldn't find her because she probably had married or moved away.
Couldn't find a name.
And-- but I had my aunt, my mother's sister, my uncle, her husband, and their son and daughter, my cousins, who both were married to Christians at the time.
And I got reacquainted with them and then I decided I would like to go to our house, see what has happened in the house with our house.
And it had changed considerably because it had been heavily bombed, that area.
And I was standing in front of the house-- and I give the name of the street and so on-- and I got my camera ready to take a picture when a man opened the front door and asked, [GERMAN].
It was are you looking for something?
And I answered, yeah, my [GERMAN].
Yes, my childhood.
I told him I had lived in the house and asked if he had a family.
The man answered that he had a wife and two daughters to which I responded that it had been the same when we lived there, my parents and two daughters.
Then I added, but we had to leave on account of Hitler.
With that comment the man closed the door.

I did not take any pictures, realizing that I quote, 'could not go home again.' But then when I went again in '89, 22 years later, there were entirely different people, different family living there, a younger couple.
The man was a scientist and the-- the woman told me that-- they opened the door right away.
My cousin was with me there, Gunter, the man.
And they opened the door, and I had a-- the companion with me, Susanne, a friend of mine, a young friend of mine, because I was allowed to bring a companion.
And she only spoke English, so the woman immediately spoke English.
And she said they had visited the United States, her husband is a scientist, and that their two sons were-- teenage sons were on vacation in France.
It was in summer.
And showed us through the house, which had been remodeled, and offered us cake and coffee and so on.
So it was an entirely different experience.
In those 22 years, things had changed in Germany.
There's a new generation-- it was a new generation.
By the government, by the local government of Stuttgart.
They did that all over.
They're still doing it all over Germany, the towns, various towns and communities are inviting former refugees back.
And my sister refused to go at that time.
Her second husband, who's also deceased now, he sort of wanted to go.
He was Hungarian and he didn't have the strong feelings she had, but she refused.
She was invited three or four times.
I even asked her then, would she go with me?
And she wouldn't.
She said she didn't want blood money.
And she had a point, but for me I felt it was closure.
I could feel that I'm finished now with Germany.
I have no desire to go back.
And we were well-treated.
Everything was paid for, the trip, the-- the hotel, all the excursions, and the opera and whatever.
It was all paid for.
But that's, you know, when I decided when my cousin took me back there again and, of course, made a difference maybe that he was with me.
But was an entirely different experience.
First trip back, yes.
Had you wanted to go but just didn't have the opportunity?
I'm kind of wondering how you related to your background.
I was conflicted.
Did I want to go?
Didn't I?
And as long-- I was, you know, in marriage and divorce and widowed and back to graduate school.
I really didn't have the money or the time, you know, to do it, and I had to work for a living.
And my parents were then sick and I couldn't-- didn't feel I could leave St.
Louis.
I was staying here.
And I was not sure-- not that sure that I wanted to.
And then I decided.
I had some letters from my relatives who are still there, and from some friends of my father's in a different city-- Darmstadt-- inviting me to come back and to stay with them.
So I decided I would do it, and I went by myself then at that time.
And as I said, it was not all pleasant and the memories were not pleasant.

And that's why I then decided in '89, when I was invited again, that I would try to make it have a more pleasant experience and then find closure and live that rather than the bad experience in-- well, I had been back. '69 I went with a girlfriend from here who had never been abroad, never been in Europe.

And she went with me and we had an experience then in-- in a place by [NON-ENGLISH], where my mother's youngest brother, the one who had been in camp and he and his wife were then in a retirement place in Bavaria.
And when-- one evening, when we were going to go back to our hotel, my friend and I, we had lost our way.
We couldn't remember exactly how to get back.
I stopped.
I said, I can ask.
I know German, after all.
I asked this couple and I said, could you tell us how to get to such and such a hotel?
And this man said, oh you speak such good German.
What-- how come?
And I said, well, you know, I used to live here in Germany.
I was born here, and I had to leave on account of Hitler.
And he said, ah, that was nothing.
[GERMAN], he said.
That was nothing. 12 years.
And I said, did you remember Hitler was there for 12 years?
And he said, that was nothing.
And so I said to my friend, let's go.
I'm-- I'm not going to wait for his answer.
I don't want to hear that.
That was in '69.
And then in '71 I was with my sister and brother-in-law.
We were touring Europe.
We rented a car in Paris and drove all through Europe.
We had an experience in Germany again.
On the autobahn, in a restaurant, where they served my brother-in-law but they didn't-- the waitress didn't want to serve my sister and me.
And--
We never did find out.
Well, we had-- well, we had a French car, Peugeot.
We didn't know that had anything to do with it or what.
But she was willing to serve my brother-in-law, who was not Jewish-- but as she had no way of knowing that actually.
We didn't know.
We did not know.
And it was rude.
We wanted to call the police, and they kept saying they were going to call the police, but they were lying.
They didn't.
So we were waiting to see if the police-- didn't come-- because we said we're not going to stay there if we can't eat.
And we eventually left, but not before we had terrible argument with these two great big Bavarians who wanted to fight my brother-in-law.
He had done nothing, and he had just had open heart surgery and you know.
So anyway my sister said, I'll never go back.
And she didn't.
Never.
And then I went in '78.
I had a humanist conference in London, and I was in Spain with a friend, visited Spain before the conference.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then told my relatives-- because the first time I went back in '67 and in '69 I was with my friend-- they did not ask me to stay in their house and they had a big house.
That was my cousins.
I found out later, because I think that nobody would know that I was Jewish.
And they put me up in a little hotel nearby.
So I wrote, at that time, in '78, that I would visit them only if they let me stay in their house.
If not I would not visit Stuttgart.
I would visit my cousin in Ulm-- it was not far from Stuttgart, the one cousin who had been married to a cousin of my father's.
Had been married and was very faithful to him, also a Christian woman.
And I would just visit her.
And then my aunt wrote, sure you can stay with us.
And I did.
And that was when all this-- when that I shouldn't tell anybody who I was, and so on.
Yes.

Well, the articles on the Holocaust and on the rescuers have been published both by the and the I had published some articles before then on mental health issues, professional issues, and in occupational therapy.
And I established a children's unit at Elgin State Hospital.
We had a number of children with psychiatric problems as young as six years old, and I had felt that they should not be with adults.
I think some were abused by some of the adult men in the units.
And so I had established a children's unit there, and I had written about that and had it published in a professional magazine.
And I had written some articles here on health in occupational therapy journals, even in the last, well, 10 years or so.
Yeah.
